To:
Deepesh Majigamkar <chartering@varitasnavigation.com>
Subject:
NSI Daily Market Report 02/01/2026
Hi Deepesh
The market has opened soft this morning following yesterday’s drops.
Elevated geopolitical tensions had supported crude oil prices as they steadily rose in early Asian trading this morning, during the first trading session in 2026. This follows after the major benchmarks had posted their worst annual performance since the steep losses in 2020 (the pandemic era), as both Brent and West Texas Intermediate benchmarks had dropped by almost 20%. Notably on Wednesday, West Texas Intermediate had stayed around $58 per barrel as traders had been closely anticipating Trump\'s stance on the alleged exchange of attacks between Russia and Ukraine, whilst also keeping a close eye on the US president\'s next action after its tensions with Iran had sparked recently. Prices had mostly been supported by the tensions between Russia and Ukraine, as well as the rapid escalation between the US and Venezuela. The head of commodities strategy at ING, Warren Patterson, had commented on the seemingless endless geopolitical tensions, saying that "What\'s really stood out in oil markets this year is the lack of volatility, particularly given the myriad geopolitical evens and supply risks". If Patterson\'s theory about geopolitical fatigue ends up to be true, oil will likely remain range bound in the start of the new year. This is based on the most pragmatic view over the efficacy of the peace deal talks between Russian and Ukraine, which we saw Putin praising Russian soldiers and describing the war as a struggle for Russia\'s homeland during his New Year\'s address that had vowed the country\'s victory in the four-year conflict. Putin\'s security council deputy, Dmitry Medvedev, had told media that "I sincerely believe that it is near", regarding Russia\'s victory in Ukraine. Notably, Putin had also said that he would review his part in the peace deal negotiations earlier this week, adding that Ukraine\'s attacks wouldn’t go unanswered. However, considering the recent remarks from Russia\'s president and his security council deputy, it doesn’t give the impression that a peace deal will be made anytime soon. Over the New Year, Zelenskyy had posted allegations on Telegram that Russia had targeted Ukraine\'s energy infrastructure, claiming that it had launched over 200 drones that spanned over seven regions, whilst Russian authorities reported that Ukraine had orchestrated drone strikes across seven regions on its energy and industrial facilities. Continued tensions between the conflicting countries hasn’t eased the uncertainty in the markets as the outcome of the war, and the effects it may continue to have, is undetermined. Meanwhile, the markets had faced additional upward pressure after the United States had tightened its measures on Venezuela\'s oil sector by imposing sanctions on four companies and associated oil tankers who have been accused of operating in Venezuela\'s sanctioned oil trade. The restrictions that were already in place had started to force the state oil company PDVSA to begin shutting its wells that produces extra-heavy crude in the Orinoco Belt since its storage space has been quickly filling up. Now, the latest measures will effectively block sanctioned vessels from entering or leaving Venezuelan ports, putting the South American\'s exports in a stronger chokehold. Rising geopolitical tensions have supported prices as risk is boosted; since the markets are currently filled with abundant supply whilst the direction of demand growth continues to be uncertain, it may be the only bullish factor that could counter the wider structural pressures that has dominated the oil markets throughout 2025.
On Wednesday, sentiment in the market took another hit after the US EIA had reported that crude oil imports in the united States had fallen to 4.9 million barrels last week, making it the lowest levels since February 2021. It was also mentioned that oil imports from Mexico had dropped to 71,000 barrels per day in the week to the 26th of December - which according to EIA data, it is Mexico\'s record lowest imports. Worries of a global glut have persisted as we go into a new year. When the data had been tallied for 2025, the IEA and the United States had predicted that production will exceed consumption by a little over 2 million barrels per day, forecasting that the oil surplus will worsen in the upcoming months as the uncertainty of global demand appears to not be robust enough to keep up with the oversupply.
Tensions between Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates had sparked recently over Yemen. Earlier this week, Saudi forces had intercepted what they claimed was an unauthorized UAE-linked shipment that was holding weapons and military equipment, allegedly plant to be transported to southern Yemen. After the Riyadh had framed it as a security breach, the Saudi-led coalition conducted an airstrike on the southern Yemeni port of Mukalla. On the other hand, Abu Dhabi had denied that it was trying to aid the separatist groups, claiming that the equipment was only intended for its own counterterrorism forces; however, the UAE ended up saying that it would pull out its remaining forces in Yemen, according to Reuters. Since Saudi Arabia and the UAE are the core of OPEC\'s decision marking, this fare up of tensions had struck worries over the effect it will have on OPEC+ overall. Even though prices had been temporarily impacted from this rise in conflict, OPEC\'s status quo doesn’t seem to be at any risk due to the fact that missiles and militias don’t have much influence on the bloc\'s stability since it\'s security is based on the cohesion between the countries within the alliance. OPEC isn’t held by shared values, shared foreign policy, or shared views on Yemen, therefore the impact on the oil markets had been very minimal, which is largely why the bloc has been able to operate despite conflicts that involves countries that are in the group. For example, Saudi Arabia and the UAE had both entered into the conflict together in 2015, which they had gradually diverged as their interests in southern Yemen had changed: whilst Riyadh prioritizes territorial unity and border security, Abu Dhabi has backed southern factions who better align with its maritime and security goals - though these differences haven’t blown up OPEC policy. Furthermore, the UAE had openly threatened to block an OPEC+ deal in 2021 because of production baselines, the UAE\'s reasoning for refusing the deal was due to feeling that its rapidly expanding capacity was being constrained unfairly. Despite this, the dispute was resolved after a compromise was made, yet this event had exposed that OPEC\'s sticking point was capacity growth versus quota discipline. Regardless of whether the Yemen noise could test diplomatic strength and security, OPEC should be able to continue operating without disruptions as long as Saudi Arabia ensures that the conflict doesn’t affect production policy, especially since the market is currently facing an abundant amount of volatility. Notably, OPEC had held onto its narrative that the market is balanced, saying that demand is strong enough to keep up with the growing supply oversupply. Yet, even as OPEC has persistently tried to avoid the global glut narrative, the warnings of a supply overhang and softer prices have dominated market sentiment and continues to apply downward pressure on prices.
